revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}

'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏}} [[Category:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}

'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏}} [[Category:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]][[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>

Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Historical documents of Japan]] [[Category:Shinto texts]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Osaka]] [[Category:Sumiyoshi Taisha]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] [[ja:住吉大社神代記]] [[sn:umiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] [[en:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles}}

However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=draft}}

== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家|12=draft}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺|12=draft}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=draft}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=draft}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=draft}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|12=draft}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=draft}}

* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家|12=draft}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺|12=draft}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=draft}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=draft}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=draft}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|12=draft}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|12=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=draft}}

35 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:住吉大社神代記 up to rev 86098345
revision.text:
{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)|参照方法=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}} 『'''住吉大社神代記'''』（すみよしたいしゃじんだいき）は、古より[[住吉大社]]に伝来し、その由来について述べた古典籍。全1巻、719行。古代史研究の上で重要な文献の1つである。[[重要文化財]]（国指定）である。公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会の住吉大社文華館に所蔵している<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ 公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会ホームページ]</ref>。
== 概要 == 『住吉大社神代記』は、住吉大社の[[神官]]が大社の由来を[[神祇官]]に言上した解文である。主要部分は祭神である[[住吉三神]]の由来と鎮座について述べた「住吉大神顕現次第｣で、これに加えて大社の神域、神宝、眷属神、各領地の四至や由来などが詳細に述べられている。『神代記』の名が史料に登場する最初の例は[[藤原定家]]の『[[明月記]]』である。藤原定家は当時住吉大社と[[四天王寺]]との間に起きた領地問題について、住吉大社側が証拠資料として『神代記』を提示したことを述べている。『神代記』はその後も史料にしばしば言及されたが、元来神代記は秘中の書として秘蔵され、社家の人間でも拝観は許されなかった。しかし、学会で注目され始めた[[明治]]以降わずかながら拝観が許可され、[[写本]]も残されるようになり、1907年（明治40年）には[[佐伯有義]]によって「住吉大社神代記事」として刊行された（『神祇全書』第3輯）。この原書（住吉大社所蔵本）が広く知られるようになったのは[[昭和]]に入ってからで、1936年（昭和11年）[[宮地直一]]によって『神代記』の原寸大の写真複製本が刊行された。さらに[[田中卓]]が本格的に研究し、1951年（昭和26年）に研究書『住吉大社神代記』を刊行した。ちなみに『住吉大社神代記』という名は仮称であり、これまでも「住吉神代記」、「住吉神社神代記」などと呼ばれ学者の間でも一定しなかった。しかし田中卓が『住吉大社神代記』の名称を強く推して以降、この名称が定着した。3年後の1954年（昭和29年）に『住吉大社神代記』は[[重要文化財]]に指定された。
== 成立 == 巻末には[[天平]]3年（731年）7月3日の日付で[[神主]]従八位下津守宿禰嶋麻呂と、[[遣唐使]]神主正六位上津守宿禰客人（[[津守吉祥]]？）の2名の撰者の名が挙げられている。さらに[[延暦]]8年（789年）8月27日の日付で、『神代記』の真正のしるしとして津守宿禰屋主ら計8名の署名が書き加えられている。これによれば成立は天平3年（731年）の撰であるが、これは従来から疑問視されており、その根拠として本記は訓点が用いられているため、成立は十世紀以前に遡れないとされる。
== 内容 == 巻頭部分に「合す」の語の後に「従三位住吉大明神大社神代記」と「住吉現神大神顕座神縁記」と2種の文献の名が挙げられており（2 - 4）、このことから上記2種類の文献を1つにまとめたものと考えられるが、本文のどの部分がいずれの文献に属したのかは不明である。住吉三神の由来について述べた「住吉大神顕現次第」は全体の6割を占めるが、主に『[[日本書紀]]』から住吉三神と関係の深い、神代、[[仲哀天皇]]、[[神功皇后]]の部分を引用した文で構成されている。しかし、全くの引用ではなく、改変、抄約されていたり、独自の伝承が挿入されているなど、その独自性がうかがえる箇所も少なからずある。 *たとえば神代では『日本書紀』本文の神話ではなく、一書の神話を採用している。しかもそれは『[[古事記]]』神話に似たものである。しかし、これは当然で、住吉三神は[[イザナミ]]の死後、[[黄泉国]]を訪れた[[イザナギ]]が[[禊]]をした際に[[ジャーティ|化生]]したためであり、イザナミが死なない本文の神話を挙げることはむしろ考えられない。ただし、天地開闢の造化神を『日本書紀』の[[国常立神]]とせずに、『古事記』と同じ[[天御中主神|天御中主尊]]としている点は注目される。なお住吉三神は、三所大神、三軍神などと称されている。 *仲哀天皇の引用部分では、父の[[日本武尊]]が天皇と呼ばれている（『[[記紀]]』ではヤマトタケルは即位しない）。また、[[成務天皇]]が祖父と呼ばれ、さらに成務天皇は孫（つまり仲哀天皇）を立てたとしている。 *『神代記』で最も有名な伝承は、神功皇后に関するもので、神功皇后が住吉大神と「密事」があり、俗に夫婦の間柄となったと、いう主旨の註記が付されている。この伝承は神功皇后と関連してしばしば取り上げられる。
== 構成 == *神殿・神戸・神域四至・大神宮・部類神・子神 *神財流代長財 *住吉大神顕現次第 *御封奉寄本記（山河奉寄本記） *胆駒山・神南備山本記 *長柄船瀬本記 *開口水門姫神社・田蓑島姫神社 *豊島郡城辺山・河辺郡為奈山 *為奈河・木津河 *荷前二処・幣帛浜等本縁 *神前審神浜 *奉寄木小島・辛島・粟島・錦刀島御厨本縁起 *周芳沙麼魚塩地領本縁 *船木等本記 *明石郡魚次浜一処 *賀胡郡阿閇津浜一処 *八神男・八神女奉供本記 *天平瓮奉本記 *奉幣時御歌本記 *雑
== 関連書 == *『日本庶民生活資料集成』 第26巻神社縁起、[[三一書房]] *田中卓著作集7 『住吉大社神代記の研究』 [[国書刊行会]]
== 脚注 == <references />
== 外部リンク == *[http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ 住吉大社神代記 神奈備]
== 関連項目 == *[[住吉大社]] - [[住吉三神]] *[[古事記]]・[[日本書紀]] *[[津守氏]]
{{デフォルトソート:すみよしたいしやしんたいき}} [[Category:日本の歴史文書]] [[Category:神道の文献]] [[Category:大阪市の重要文化財]] [[Category:住吉大社]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[住吉大社神代記]] to [[Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)|参照方法=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:50 (UTC)}} 『'''住吉大社神代記'''』（すみよしたいしゃじんだいき）は、古より[[住吉大社]]に伝来し、その由来について述べた古典籍。全1巻、719行。古代史研究の上で重要な文献の1つである。[[重要文化財]]（国指定）である。公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会の住吉大社文華館に所蔵している<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ 公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会ホームページ]</ref>。
== 概要 == 『住吉大社神代記』は、住吉大社の[[神官]]が大社の由来を[[神祇官]]に言上した解文である。主要部分は祭神である[[住吉三神]]の由来と鎮座について述べた「住吉大神顕現次第｣で、これに加えて大社の神域、神宝、眷属神、各領地の四至や由来などが詳細に述べられている。『神代記』の名が史料に登場する最初の例は[[藤原定家]]の『[[明月記]]』である。藤原定家は当時住吉大社と[[四天王寺]]との間に起きた領地問題について、住吉大社側が証拠資料として『神代記』を提示したことを述べている。『神代記』はその後も史料にしばしば言及されたが、元来神代記は秘中の書として秘蔵され、社家の人間でも拝観は許されなかった。しかし、学会で注目され始めた[[明治]]以降わずかながら拝観が許可され、[[写本]]も残されるようになり、1907年（明治40年）には[[佐伯有義]]によって「住吉大社神代記事」として刊行された（『神祇全書』第3輯）。この原書（住吉大社所蔵本）が広く知られるようになったのは[[昭和]]に入ってからで、1936年（昭和11年）[[宮地直一]]によって『神代記』の原寸大の写真複製本が刊行された。さらに[[田中卓]]が本格的に研究し、1951年（昭和26年）に研究書『住吉大社神代記』を刊行した。ちなみに『住吉大社神代記』という名は仮称であり、これまでも「住吉神代記」、「住吉神社神代記」などと呼ばれ学者の間でも一定しなかった。しかし田中卓が『住吉大社神代記』の名称を強く推して以降、この名称が定着した。3年後の1954年（昭和29年）に『住吉大社神代記』は[[重要文化財]]に指定された。
== 成立 == 巻末には[[天平]]3年（731年）7月3日の日付で[[神主]]従八位下津守宿禰嶋麻呂と、[[遣唐使]]神主正六位上津守宿禰客人（[[津守吉祥]]？）の2名の撰者の名が挙げられている。さらに[[延暦]]8年（789年）8月27日の日付で、『神代記』の真正のしるしとして津守宿禰屋主ら計8名の署名が書き加えられている。これによれば成立は天平3年（731年）の撰であるが、これは従来から疑問視されており、その根拠として本記は訓点が用いられているため、成立は十世紀以前に遡れないとされる。
== 内容 == 巻頭部分に「合す」の語の後に「従三位住吉大明神大社神代記」と「住吉現神大神顕座神縁記」と2種の文献の名が挙げられており（2 - 4）、このことから上記2種類の文献を1つにまとめたものと考えられるが、本文のどの部分がいずれの文献に属したのかは不明である。住吉三神の由来について述べた「住吉大神顕現次第」は全体の6割を占めるが、主に『[[日本書紀]]』から住吉三神と関係の深い、神代、[[仲哀天皇]]、[[神功皇后]]の部分を引用した文で構成されている。しかし、全くの引用ではなく、改変、抄約されていたり、独自の伝承が挿入されているなど、その独自性がうかがえる箇所も少なからずある。 *たとえば神代では『日本書紀』本文の神話ではなく、一書の神話を採用している。しかもそれは『[[古事記]]』神話に似たものである。しかし、これは当然で、住吉三神は[[イザナミ]]の死後、[[黄泉国]]を訪れた[[イザナギ]]が[[禊]]をした際に[[ジャーティ|化生]]したためであり、イザナミが死なない本文の神話を挙げることはむしろ考えられない。ただし、天地開闢の造化神を『日本書紀』の[[国常立神]]とせずに、『古事記』と同じ[[天御中主神|天御中主尊]]としている点は注目される。なお住吉三神は、三所大神、三軍神などと称されている。 *仲哀天皇の引用部分では、父の[[日本武尊]]が天皇と呼ばれている（『[[記紀]]』ではヤマトタケルは即位しない）。また、[[成務天皇]]が祖父と呼ばれ、さらに成務天皇は孫（つまり仲哀天皇）を立てたとしている。 *『神代記』で最も有名な伝承は、神功皇后に関するもので、神功皇后が住吉大神と「密事」があり、俗に夫婦の間柄となったと、いう主旨の註記が付されている。この伝承は神功皇后と関連してしばしば取り上げられる。
== 構成 == *神殿・神戸・神域四至・大神宮・部類神・子神 *神財流代長財 *住吉大神顕現次第 *御封奉寄本記（山河奉寄本記） *胆駒山・神南備山本記 *長柄船瀬本記 *開口水門姫神社・田蓑島姫神社 *豊島郡城辺山・河辺郡為奈山 *為奈河・木津河 *荷前二処・幣帛浜等本縁 *神前審神浜 *奉寄木小島・辛島・粟島・錦刀島御厨本縁起 *周芳沙麼魚塩地領本縁 *船木等本記 *明石郡魚次浜一処 *賀胡郡阿閇津浜一処 *八神男・八神女奉供本記 *天平瓮奉本記 *奉幣時御歌本記 *雑
== 関連書 == *『日本庶民生活資料集成』 第26巻神社縁起、[[三一書房]] *田中卓著作集7 『住吉大社神代記の研究』 [[国書刊行会]]
== 脚注 == <references />
== 外部リンク == *[http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ 住吉大社神代記 神奈備]
== 関連項目 == *[[住吉大社]] - [[住吉三神]] *[[古事記]]・[[日本書紀]] *[[津守氏]]
{{デフォルトソート:すみよしたいしやしんたいき}} [[Category:日本の歴史文書]] [[Category:神道の文献]] [[Category:大阪市の重要文化財]] [[Category:住吉大社]]

'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[sn:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] [[ja:住吉大社神代記]] [[sn:umiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]] [[en:Sumiyoshi Taisha Shrine Chronicles]]

'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|ja|藤原定家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennoji|ja|四天王寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|qq=draft|qq=draft}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Envoy to Tang China|ja|遣唐使|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chuai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Empress {{ill|Jingu|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|ja|イザナギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|qq=}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|qq=}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|qq=}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Taisha|qq=}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|ja|住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Sanjin|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi sanjin|qq=}} * {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}
