revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381815]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-taisha Jindaiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]], detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]]. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by [[:en:Fujiwara no Teika|Fujiwara no Teika]], who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennoji]]. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Envoy to Tang China]]. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], with references to the mythological age, [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chuai]], and Empress [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingu]]. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] * [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}

This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381815]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-taisha Jindaiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]], detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]]. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by [[:en:Fujiwara no Teika|Fujiwara no Teika]], who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennoji]]. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Envoy to Tang China]]. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], with references to the mythological age, [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chuai]], and Empress [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingu]]. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381815]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-taisha Jindaiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]], detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]] and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]], explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]]. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by [[:en:Fujiwara no Teika|Fujiwara no Teika]], who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and [[:en:Shitennō-ji|Shitennoji]]. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible [[:en:Japanese missions to Tang China|Envoy to Tang China]]. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], with references to the mythological age, [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chuai]], and Empress [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingu]]. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of [[:en:Izanagi|Izanagi]] following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sumiyoshi sanjin]] * [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|住吉大社神代記|version=86098345|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381815]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-taisha Jindaiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|WD=Q339859|lt=Shitennoji}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Envoy to Tang China}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chuai}}, and Empress {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Jingu}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|住吉大社神代記|version=86098345|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381815]] Wikidata name: Sumiyoshi-taisha Jindaiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:36, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|WD=Q339859|lt=Shitennoji}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Envoy to Tang China}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chuai}}, and Empress {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Jingu}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:住吉大社神代記]]
'''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki''' (住吉大社神代記) is an ancient text that has been preserved by {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, detailing its origins. The document comprises a single volume with 719 lines and is regarded as an essential resource for the study of ancient Japanese history. It has been designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} and is housed in the Sumiyoshi Taisha Bunka-kan under the management of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja|公益財団法人住吉名勝保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[https://sumiyoshimeishohozonkai.com/activity/96/ Sumiyoshi Meisho Hozonkai Foundation homepage]</ref>
== Overview ==
The ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' was authored as a report submitted by the shrine priests of Sumiyoshi Taisha to the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, explaining the shrine's origins. The central part, titled "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" (住吉大神顕現次第), discusses the origins and enshrinement of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additional sections cover the shrine's precincts, sacred treasures, associated deities, and the boundaries and histories of its domains.
The text is first mentioned in historical records in {{ill|Meigetsuki|lt=The Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}} by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who noted its use as evidence in a territorial dispute between Sumiyoshi Taisha and {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|WD=Q339859|lt=Shitennoji|1=Shitennō-ji}}. Though frequently referenced in later sources, it was kept secret, even from most shrine members, until the Meiji period when it began to attract scholarly attention. In 1907, a transcription titled "Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki Article" was published by {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}} in the third volume of ''Shingi Zensho.'' In 1936, {{ill|Miyachi Naoichi|ja|宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyachi Naoichi|1=Naokazu Miyaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453535}} produced a photographic reproduction of the original text. Subsequently, {{ill|Tanaka Taku|ja|田中卓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Taku|1=Takashi Tanaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11575370}} undertook a detailed study and published his research in 1951. Since then, the name ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki'' has been widely adopted, following Tanaka's advocacy. The text was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1954.
== Composition ==
The text concludes with the date of July 3, 731 (Tenpyo 3) and lists two authors: Jushichiinojō Tsunomori no Sukune Shimamaro, a shrine priest, and Jushirō Tsunomori no Sukune Kyakujin, a possible {{ill|Japanese missions to Tang China|en|Japanese missions to Tang China|WD=Q262529|lt=Envoy to Tang China|1=Japanese missions to Tang China}}. An additional note dated August 27, 789 (Enryaku 8) includes the signatures of eight more individuals to certify the text's authenticity. However, scholars have questioned this date, noting that the text contains annotated readings that suggest it could not have been compiled before the 10th century.
== Contents ==
The introduction mentions two separate texts: the ''Sumiyoshi Great Deity Jindaiki'' (従三位住吉大明神大社神代記) and the ''Record of the Origins of the Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity'' (住吉現神大神顕座神縁記). It is thought that the ''Jindaiki'' combines these two works, though it is unclear how the content is divided. "The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity" forms about 60% of the text, largely quoting from the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, with references to the mythological age, {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chuai|1=Emperor Chūai}}, and Empress {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}. While heavily reliant on the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it also includes unique elements such as modifications and independent traditions.
* The section on the Age of the Gods uses a variant myth closer to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'' rather than the main ''Nihon Shoki'' text. Notably, it aligns the creation of the Sumiyoshi Sanjin with the purification (禊) of {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} following his visit to {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 黄泉|1=Yomi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1142558}}, consistent with ''Kojiki.'' However, the primordial deity is identified as {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}}, following the ''Kojiki,'' instead of the ''Nihon Shoki's'' {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi|lt=Kuninotokotachi|ja|国常立神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuninotokotachi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国之常立神}}. * Emperor Chuai's section portrays his father, {{ill|Prince Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, as an emperor, which contradicts the ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative. Emperor Seimu is referred to as Chuai's grandfather and is said to have chosen his grandson to succeed him. * The text's most famous passage details a "secret union" between Empress Jingu and the Sumiyoshi Deity, implying a marital relationship. This unique interpretation has sparked considerable interest in the connection between the Empress and the deity.
== Structure ==
The text is divided into several thematic sections: * Shrine precincts, divine households, and boundaries * Divine treasures and long-standing offerings * The Manifestation of the Sumiyoshi Great Deity * Records of mountain and river offerings * Records of Mt. Tamakuma and Kamunabi Mountain * Nagara Funase and other records * Records of associated shrines * Descriptions of rivers, fields, and territories * Ritual records and associated traditions * Miscellaneous sections
== Related Literature ==
* ''Nihon Shomin Seikatsu Shiryo Shusei,'' Vol. 26, {{ill|Sanja Shobo|ja|三一書房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanja Shobo|1=San-ichi Publishing Co., Ltd.|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10865224}} * Taku Tanaka, ''Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki no Kenkyu,'' {{ill|Kokusho Kankokai|ja|国書刊行会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusho Kankokai|1=Kokusho Kankōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420946}}
== Notes == <references />
== External Links == * [http://kamnavi.jp/sumiyosi/ Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki - Kamnavi] == See Also == * {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} * {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} * {{ill|Tsumori clan|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}
